title: Burns
subtitle: Fire, hot water, chemical burns
steps: Move the person away from the heat source immediately. Do NOT touch them if they are still in contact with electricity.
Cool the burn under COOL (not cold/icy) running water for at least 10–20 minutes. Start this as soon as possible.
Do NOT apply toothpaste, butter, shea butter, egg white, or any home remedy — these cause infection.
Remove jewellery (rings, bracelets) and clothing near the burn, unless it is stuck to the skin.
Cover the burn loosely with a clean, non-fluffy cloth or cling wrap. Do NOT use cotton wool.
Do NOT burst any blisters — they protect against infection.
If the burn is larger than the person's palm, on the face/hands/feet/genitals, or the skin looks white/black/leathery — GO TO THE HOSPITAL IMMEDIATELY.
warnings: Never use ice directly on a burn.
Never apply toothpaste, shea butter, or butter.
Never pop blisters.
whenToGoToHospital: Any burn larger than the person's palm, burns on face/hands/genitals, deep burns, chemical burns, or burns with difficulty breathing.

title: Choking
subtitle: Adult, child, and infant
steps: Ask the person 'Are you choking?' If they can speak, cough, or breathe — encourage them to keep coughing. Do NOT interfere.
If they CANNOT speak, cough, or breathe: Stand behind them. Give 5 firm back blows between the shoulder blades with the heel of your hand.

If back blows don't clear it: Perform 5 abdominal thrusts (Heimlich manoeuvre). Place one fist just above their belly button, grab your fist with your other hand, and thrust inward and upward sharply.
Alternate 5 back blows and 5 abdominal thrusts until the object comes out or the person can breathe.
If they become unconscious, lower them gently to the ground and call for help. Begin CPR if you know how.

FOR INFANTS (under 1 year): Lay face-down on your forearm, support the head, give 5 gentle back blows. Then flip face-up and give 5 chest thrusts with 2 fingers on the breastbone. NEVER do abdominal thrusts on infants.
warnings: Never do abdominal thrusts on infants under 1 year.
Never do a blind finger sweep — you may push the object deeper.
Do not interfere if they can still cough forcefully.
whenToGoToHospital: After any choking episode, especially if the person lost consciousness, is still having difficulty breathing, or if you are unsure whether the object was fully cleared.

title: Bleeding / Wounds
subtitle: Cuts, lacerations, open wounds
steps: Protect yourself — wear gloves if available, or use a plastic bag. Avoid direct contact with another person's blood.
Apply DIRECT pressure to the wound using a clean cloth, pad, or bandage. Press firmly and hold — do NOT keep lifting to check.
If blood soaks through the cloth, add MORE cloth on top. Do NOT remove the first cloth.
Elevate the wounded area above the heart level if possible (e.g., raise the arm or leg).
Maintain pressure for at least 10–15 minutes. Do not release too early.
Once bleeding slows, secure the dressing with a bandage. Do not tie so tightly that it cuts off circulation.
Seek medical help if: the wound is deep, gaping wide, has a foreign object in it, or bleeding does not stop after 15 minutes of firm pressure.
warnings: Do NOT remove an embedded object from a wound — it may be plugging a blood vessel.
Do NOT use a tourniquet unless you are trained to do so.
Do NOT apply salt, toothpaste, or any substances to the wound.
whenToGoToHospital: Wound is deep, gaping, won't stop bleeding after 15 minutes, has a foreign object embedded, or involves the face/eye/chest/abdomen.

title: Snake Bite
subtitle: Venomous or unknown snake
steps: Keep calm and keep the person as still as possible. Movement pumps venom through the body faster.
Move the person away from the snake. Do NOT try to catch or kill the snake.
Lay the person down with the bitten limb BELOW the level of the heart if possible.
Remove any tight clothing, watches, rings, or jewellery near the bite — swelling will occur.
Loosely cover the bite with a clean bandage. Do NOT suck the venom or cut the wound.
Do NOT apply a tourniquet, electric shock, or ice to the bite.
Note the time of the bite and describe the snake to medical staff if possible (colour, size, pattern).
GET TO THE HOSPITAL IMMEDIATELY. Antivenom must be given by medical professionals.
warnings: Never suck the venom — it does not help and can harm you.
Never cut the bite wound.
Never apply a tourniquet or tight bandage.
Never use traditional herbs or apply anything to the wound.
whenToGoToHospital: ALL snake bites should be treated as emergencies. Go to the hospital IMMEDIATELY even if you are unsure if the snake was venomous.

title: Drowning
subtitle: Near-drowning, water rescue
steps: Call for help immediately. Do NOT enter deep water yourself unless you are a trained swimmer/rescuer.
If safe to do so, throw a rope, life ring, or any floating object to the person. Pull them to safety.
Once the person is out of the water, check if they are breathing. Tilt their head back and look for chest rise.
If they are NOT breathing: Start rescue breathing — give 5 initial rescue breaths. Pinch the nose, seal your mouth over theirs, and breathe into them until you see the chest rise.
If there is still no normal breathing: Begin CPR. Push down hard and fast on the centre of the chest (100–120 times per minute), alternating with rescue breaths if trained.
Keep the person warm — wet clothing causes rapid heat loss. Remove wet clothes if possible and cover with a dry blanket.
Even if the person seems recovered, take them to the hospital — delayed lung complications can occur hours later.
warnings: Do not leave the person alone, even if they seem fine initially.
Do not waste time trying to drain water from the lungs — start CPR first.
Secondary drowning can happen hours later — always seek medical evaluation.
whenToGoToHospital: ALL near-drowning cases must go to the hospital, even if the person appears to have recovered.

title: Seizures / Convulsions
subtitle: Epilepsy, febrile seizures
steps: Stay calm. Time the seizure — if it lasts more than 5 minutes, call for emergency help immediately.
Clear the area around the person — remove hard or sharp objects they could hit.
Do NOT hold the person down or restrain their movements.
Do NOT put anything in their mouth — they CANNOT swallow their tongue. Putting something in the mouth can break their teeth or your fingers.
Gently turn the person on their side (recovery position) to prevent choking on saliva or vomit.
Cushion their head with something soft — a folded jacket or blanket.
After the seizure stops, keep them on their side, speak calmly, and stay with them until they are fully conscious.
The person may be confused and tired after — this is normal. Let them rest.
warnings: Never put anything in the person's mouth during a seizure.
Never restrain or hold them down forcefully.
Do not give water until they are fully conscious.
whenToGoToHospital: Seizure lasts more than 5 minutes, the person does not regain consciousness, this is their first seizure, they are pregnant, or they are injured during the seizure.

title: Fainting / Unconscious
subtitle: Sudden loss of consciousness
steps: Do NOT leave the person alone. If they are falling, try to support them gently to prevent injury from hitting the ground.
Lay them on their back on a flat surface. Loosen any tight clothing around the neck, chest, or waist.
Raise their legs above the level of their heart (about 30cm / 12 inches off the ground) to help blood return to the brain.
If they do NOT regain consciousness within 1–2 minutes, place them in the recovery position (on their side).
Check for breathing. If they are not breathing normally, begin CPR.
Check for any obvious cause: low blood sugar, heat, dehydration, injury, allergic reaction.
When they regain consciousness, let them lie still for a few minutes. Then help them sit up slowly — moving too fast can cause fainting again.
Offer water or something sweet (like juice) if they are fully conscious and can swallow safely.
warnings: Do not give food or drink to an unconscious person.
Do not slap the person to wake them.
Do not leave them alone until they are fully recovered.
whenToGoToHospital: Does not regain consciousness within 2 minutes, has difficulty breathing, was injured in the fall, has chest pain, or has a history of heart problems.

title: Fractures / Sprains
subtitle: Broken bones, twisted joints
steps: Do NOT try to straighten a broken bone — you can cause more damage and intense pain.
Immobilise the injured area in the position you find it. Support the area above AND below the injury.
Improvise a splint using a rigid item (stick, rolled newspaper, board) padded with cloth. Secure it gently — not too tight.
If there is an open wound (bone visible or skin broken), cover it loosely with a clean cloth. Do NOT push the bone back in.
Apply a cold pack or ice wrapped in cloth to help reduce swelling and pain. Never place ice directly on skin.
Elevate the injured limb if possible to reduce swelling.
For suspected spinal injury: Do NOT move the person. Keep their head, neck, and spine as still as possible. Call for help.
Get to the hospital for X-ray and proper treatment.
warnings: Never try to realign or push a broken bone back.
Never move someone with a suspected neck or spine injury.
Do not apply heat in the first 48 hours.
whenToGoToHospital: All suspected fractures need medical evaluation and X-ray. Go immediately if there is an open wound, loss of feeling, suspected spinal injury, or severe deformity.

title: Electric Shock
subtitle: Electrical injury, lightning strike
steps: DO NOT touch the person while they are still in contact with the electrical source — you will be shocked too.

Turn off the power at the switch, mains box, or circuit breaker IMMEDIATELY. If you cannot turn off the power, use a dry wooden broom handle or plastic chair to push the source away — never use anything metal or wet.
Once they are free from the source and it is SAFE, check for response. Call for emergency help.
If unconscious and not breathing normally, begin CPR immediately.
If conscious, lay them down, check for burns — entry and exit wounds may appear as small marks.
Cover any burn areas with a clean cloth. Do NOT apply water, creams, or any substances.
Keep the person warm and prevent shock (lay them flat, elevate legs if no spinal injury suspected).
GO TO THE HOSPITAL — internal injuries from electrical shock can be serious even when external injuries appear minor.
warnings: Never touch the person if they are still connected to the power source.
Never use metal objects to disconnect the source.
Never assume internal injuries are absent just because external burns look small.
whenToGoToHospital: ALL electrical shock cases must go to the hospital, regardless of how the person feels. Internal cardiac and organ damage can be delayed.

title: Poisoning / Food Poisoning
subtitle: Ingested substances, food poisoning
steps: Identify what was swallowed and when. Keep the container or packaging if available to show medical staff.
Do NOT induce vomiting unless specifically instructed by a medical professional or poison control — vomiting some chemicals can cause more damage.
If the person is conscious and alert, give water or milk to dilute the poison ONLY if instructed.
For food poisoning: let the person rest. If they can keep fluids down, encourage oral rehydration (ORS, or a mix of water + salt + sugar). Monitor for signs of dehydration.
Watch for danger signs: difficulty breathing, seizures, severe abdominal pain, loss of consciousness, or blood in vomit.
If the poison got onto the skin or eyes: rinse immediately with large amounts of cool running water for at least 15–20 minutes.
If inhaled chemicals cause difficulty breathing: move the person to fresh air immediately.
Seek medical attention for all intentional poisoning, unknown substances, or worsening symptoms.
warnings: Never induce vomiting without medical advice — especially for acid, alkali, or petroleum products.
Never give anything by mouth if the person is unconscious.
Bring the poison container to the hospital.
whenToGoToHospital: All suspected poisonings with unknown substances, any poisoning in a child, or when symptoms include difficulty breathing, seizures, unconsciousness, or chest pain.

title: Allergic Reaction
subtitle: Anaphylaxis, severe allergy
steps: Identify and REMOVE the trigger (food, insect sting, medication) if still present and safe to do so.
Watch for signs of SEVERE reaction (anaphylaxis): difficulty breathing, swelling of the face/throat/tongue, severe hives, dizziness, or collapse.
If they have a prescribed EpiPen (adrenaline auto injector), administer it immediately into the outer thigh — even through clothing. Call for emergency help.
If no EpiPen is available and there are severe symptoms: lay them down with legs raised (unless having difficulty breathing — then sit them upright), call for emergency help immediately.
If the reaction is mild (skin hives, itching, runny nose): an antihistamine (like chlorphenamine) may help — follow package dosing instructions.
Keep the person calm and still. Stress worsens anaphylaxis.
Even after using an EpiPen and symptoms improve, go to the hospital — symptoms can return within hours.
Note exactly what the person was exposed to; tell the medical staff.
warnings: Anaphylaxis is life-threatening. Do not wait to see if it gets better — act fast.
Do not give anything by mouth if the person has throat swelling or difficulty swallowing.
The effect of an EpiPen is temporary — hospital treatment is still needed.
whenToGoToHospital: Any allergic reaction with breathing difficulty, throat swelling, collapse, or loss of consciousness is a medical emergency. Go IMMEDIATELY.

title: Fever in Children
subtitle: High temperature, febrile seizures
steps: Check the child's temperature. A fever is generally a temperature above 38°C (100.4°F). Use a thermometer if available.
Keep the child lightly dressed — remove heavy clothing. Do not wrap them in thick blankets.
Give the child paracetamol (Panadol/acetaminophen) at the correct dose for their weight and age. Check the label carefully.
Encourage the child to drink plenty of fluids — water, breastmilk, oral rehydration solution.
Sponge the child with lukewarm (not cold) water if they are very uncomfortable. Focus on their forearms, armpits, forehead, and neck.
Keep the room cool and well-ventilated.
Monitor closely for warning signs: difficulty breathing, rash, stiff neck, persistent vomiting, non-stop crying, or seizures.
If the child has a febrile seizure: keep them safe (see Seizures guide), lay them on their side, and go to the hospital after it stops.
warnings: Never use aspirin for children under 16 — it can cause a serious condition called Reye's syndrome.
Never use ice-cold water for sponging — it can cause shivering which raises the temperature.
Never leave a feverish child unattended.
whenToGoToHospital: Child under 3 months with any fever, temperature above 39.5°C, fever lasting more than 3 days, child is unusually drowsy or difficult to wake, has a seizure, rash, stiff neck, or difficulty breathing.

title: Nosebleeds
subtitle: Nasal haemorrhage
steps: Have the person sit upright and lean FORWARD slightly — not backward. Leaning back causes blood to flow down the throat.
Pinch the SOFT part of the nose (below the hard bony bridge) firmly between the index finger and thumb.
Breathe through the mouth. Hold continuous firm pressure for 10–15 minutes without releasing to check.
Apply a cold compress or ice pack (wrapped in cloth) to the bridge of the nose and back of the neck to help constrict blood vessels.
After the bleeding stops, advise the person NOT to blow their nose, sniff, or pick their nose for at least a few hours.
Avoid bending over or physical activity for the rest of the day.
warnings: Never have the person tilt their head back — this causes blood to be swallowed.
Do not pack the nose with cotton wool or cloth and leave it in.
If the blood is very heavy or spurting, go to the hospital.
whenToGoToHospital: Heavy bleeding that doesn't stop after 20 minutes of firm pressure, result of a head injury, the person takes blood thinners, or the person becomes pale, dizzy, or faint.
